title: Visiting Someone's Home
situation: You're visiting your friend's parents in Lucknow for the first time. They've invited you for tea and snacks.

phrases.hindi: namaste aunty ji, kaise hain aap?
phrases.context: 'aunty/uncle' + 'ji' is how you address friends' parents — never use first names. 'ji' adds respect to any name or title.
phrases.pronunciation: na-mas-TE AUN-ty ji, KAI-se hain AAP?

phrases.hindi: yeh chhota sa gift hai, aapke liye laaya hoon
phrases.english: Here's a small gift, I brought it for you
phrases.context: Always bring something — sweets, fruit, or flowers. 'chhota sa' (just a small thing) downplays the gift, a polite Indian convention.
phrases.pronunciation: YEH chho-TA sa GIFT hai, AAP-ke li-YE LAA-ya hoon

phrases.english: Should I take off my shoes here?
phrases.context: Asking shows you know the custom. Shoes always come off before entering — outside the door or in a small entry area.

phrases.hindi: aaiye, baithiye — chai chadha rahi hoon
phrases.english: Come, sit — I'm putting the tea on
phrases.context: What the host says. 'chai chadhaana' literally = put tea up — to start brewing. Refusing chai is almost rude.
phrases.pronunciation: AA-iye, BAI-thi-ye — CHAI cha-DHA ra-HI hoon

phrases.hindi: bas paani chalega, takleef mat kijiye
phrases.english: Just water is fine, please don't go to any trouble
phrases.context: Standard polite-refusal opener — you'll be ignored, but it's the courteous move. 'takleef' = trouble/inconvenience.
phrases.pronunciation: BAS paa-NI cha-LE-ga, tak-LEEF mat KI-ji-ye

phrases.hindi: arre, thoda thoda toh lijiye, khaas aapke liye banaaya hai
phrases.english: Oh come on, have a little at least, I made it specially for you
phrases.context: The host's insistence move — guaranteed to happen. 'thoda thoda' (just a little) makes refusal harder. You should give in.
phrases.pronunciation: AR-re, tho-DA tho-DA toh LI-ji-ye, KHAAS AAP-ke li-YE ba-NAA-ya hai

phrases.hindi: bahut tasty hai, aapke haath mein toh jaadu hai
phrases.english: It's so tasty, you've got magic in your hands
phrases.context: 'haath mein jaadu' (magic in your hands) is the highest compliment to a cook. Always compliment — host fishes for it.
phrases.pronunciation: ba-HUT TASTY hai, AAP-ke HAATH mein toh JAA-du hai

phrases.hindi: bas ji, ab nahi — pet bhar gaya sach mein
phrases.english: That's enough, no more — I'm actually full
phrases.context: 'bas ji' = enough please. 'sach mein' (truly) — you need to swear you're full, otherwise they'll keep loading your plate.
phrases.pronunciation: BAS ji, AB na-HI — PET bhar GA-ya SACH mein

phrases.hindi: ab chalta hoon, der ho rahi hai
phrases.english: I'll head off now, it's getting late
phrases.context: Polite exit. 'chalta/chalti hoon' literally = I'm walking. Standard Indian goodbye when leaving someone's home.
phrases.pronunciation: AB chal-TA hoon, DER ho ra-HI hai

phrases.hindi: zaroor aaiyega phir se, ghar apna hi samjhiye
phrases.english: Definitely come again, treat this home as your own
phrases.context: What the host says on goodbye. 'ghar apna hi samjhiye' = consider this house yours — the warmest Indian goodbye.
phrases.pronunciation: za-ROOR aa-IYE-ga PHIR se, GHAR AP-na hi sam-JHI-ye

grammar_notes: Polite imperative '-iye' (e.g., baithiye = please sit, lijiye = please take) is the 'aap' form of commands — always use with elders and friends' parents
'-iyega' adds even more politeness/futurity: 'aaiyega' = please do come (future polite). Use it when inviting someone back
'ji' is a universal respect marker — attach to anyone's name or title (uncle ji, Sharma ji, didi ji). Never optional with elders
Compound verbs of completion: 'utaar dena' (take off completely), 'kha lena' (eat up), 'pi lena' (drink up). The second verb adds finality/politeness
Reduplication softens commands: 'thoda thoda lijiye' (have just a little) is gentler than 'lijiye' (take it). Same with 'jaldi jaldi' (quickly) — the doubled form is friendlier
culture_notes: Always bring something — mithai (sweets), fruit, or flowers. Showing up empty-handed signals you don't know the etiquette
Touching the feet (charan-sparsh) of older relatives or very senior elders is traditional. They'll bless you with 'jeete raho' (live long). Skip with strangers' parents — namaste is fine
Refusing food/drink the first time is the polite default — the host will insist, then you accept. Refusing completely can offend
Praising the cook explicitly is expected, not optional. 'Aunty ji, kya banaya hai!' (what a dish you've made!) lands well
When you say 'ab chalta hoon' (I'll leave now), expect 15-30 more minutes of conversation before you actually leave

skill_breakdown.explanation: The 'aap' form of commands. Take any verb stem and add '-iye' for polite present command or '-iyega' for polite future/invitation. Use with elders, strangers, or anyone you'd call 'aap'. Irregular: lena→lijiye, dena→dijiye, peena→pijiye, karna→kijiye.

more_examples.english: please give me a glass of water
see you again, please take care

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi hospitality follows a script: greeting → seating → offering → insisting → praising → leaving. Master these phrases and you can navigate any home visit with grace.

more_examples.hindi: kuch lenge aap? chai ya coffee?
more_examples.english: will you have something? tea or coffee?

more_examples.hindi: bilkul ghar jaisa lag raha hai
more_examples.english: it feels just like home (compliment to host)

more_examples.hindi: aap ne toh poori dawat kar di
more_examples.english: you've laid out a whole feast
do come to our home sometime too

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Aap' is the formal/respectful 'you' — always use with friends' parents, anyone older, or strangers. 'Ji' is a respect particle: attach to names (Sharma ji), titles (uncle ji), or use alone (ji haan = yes-respectful, ji nahi = no-respectful). When in doubt, default to aap + ji.

practice_prompt: You're visiting your friend Rohan's parents in Lucknow for the first time. You've brought a box of sweets. His mother is offering you chai and homemade samosas. Greet them respectfully, hand over the gift, navigate the offer-refuse-accept ritual, compliment the food, and make a graceful exit after about 20 minutes of conversation. The mother is warm and will keep insisting.
references: Snell & Weightman, 'Teach Yourself Hindi' — Ch. on polite imperatives and the 'aap' register
Afroz Taj, 'A Door Into Hindi' (UNC) — Module on hospitality and home visits
McGregor, 'Outline of Hindi Grammar' — Compound verb usage for politeness
